title: Chamomile eye compress for tired or irritated eyes
subtitle: A cooling compress for sore, puffy eyes
excerpt: A cooled chamomile infusion used as a compress for tired, irritated, or puffy eyes. Applied as a soaked pad held over the closed eyes for five minutes. A traditional remedy with a long domestic history and no significant safety concerns at normal household use.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), chamomile entry, anti-inflammatory and soothing action, traditional use as an eye wash. Traditional household use documented across European domestic medicine texts.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh for each session. The compress liquid must be used on the day it is prepared and should never be stored.

glossaryTerms.definition: A clean cloth or pad soaked in a cooled herbal infusion and held over closed eyes to reduce irritation, puffiness, or fatigue.

content.text: A cooled chamomile infusion soaked into a clean cloth and held over closed eyes for five minutes. The

soothes the periorbital skin and eyelids, helpful for eyes tired after long hours at a screen, eyes that are puffy after a poor night's sleep, or mild allergic irritation around the eye area. This is a well-established domestic remedy with a long European household history. Chamomile's anti-inflammatory and soothing properties are well documented; at the concentrations used in a compress (an infusion held over the closed lid, not applied into the eye), the main risk is the Asteraceae allergy shared by some readers.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy: chamomile is in the Asteraceae (daisy) family. If you have a known allergy to ragweed, chrysanthemum, calendula, or related plants, do not use chamomile compresses on or near the eyes.

This is a compress for the closed eyelid and periorbital skin. Do not apply liquid directly into the eye, this is not an eye wash or eye drop preparation.

Infection: do not use this preparation if the eye is red, producing discharge, or painful, these are signs of conjunctivitis or another infection that needs medical assessment, not a herbal compress.

Contact lenses: remove contact lenses before using any compress near the eyes. Do not reinsert until any liquid has been completely washed from the face.

See your doctor for: any change in vision, pain inside the eye, a foreign body in the eye, red eye with discharge, or eye symptoms that are worsening. Eye problems that go beyond tiredness and puffiness need prompt medical assessment.

items.prepNote: boiled, then cooled completely to room temperature before use

content.text: You will also need two clean pads, one for each eye, of soft, clean cotton wool or a cotton cloth. Use a separate pad for each eye to avoid cross-contamination if there is any irritation.

Place {{dried-chamomile-flowers}} of dried chamomile flowers in the teapot. Pour {{water}} of just-boiled water over, put the lid on, and steep for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a clean jug. Leave to cool completely to room temperature, this is essential. Do not use a warm compress on the eyes; a cool or lukewarm compress is more soothing and safer for the delicate periorbital skin.

Lie back. Soak a clean cotton pad in the cooled infusion and press gently but firmly against the excess liquid. Place the soaked pad over a closed eye. Repeat with a second pad for the other eye. Rest with the compresses in place for five to ten minutes. Discard the pads after use.

One session of five to ten minutes, once or twice daily, for as long as the tiredness or puffiness persists. Use fresh liquid for each session. If eye irritation is not improving after three days, or is accompanied by any other symptoms, see your GP or optician.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not use for red, painful, or discharging eyes. Skip if you have an Asteraceae allergy. Do not apply liquid directly into the eye. See your doctor or optician for any eye symptoms beyond tiredness and puffiness, early conjunctivitis, blepharitis, and other eye conditions need proper diagnosis.
